html:
WEBJF is not the old consumer Webjet story anymore. It is the OTC line for Web Travel Group, the ASX:WEB company that kept WebBeds after the 2024 demerger of Webjet Group. The market is still partly trading the old label: “Australian online travel agent, post-COVID, exposed to Google Flights and consumer complaints.” The current business is much cleaner: a pure-play global B2B hotel marketplace connecting hotels and ground-product suppliers with travel sellers.

The variant perception is that WebBeds is not a weak OTA. It is a scaled B2B distribution layer in a fragmented market. The company says the B2B travel market is about US$96 billion in TTV, with WebBeds serving buyers across a global footprint. In 1H26, WebBeds produced A$3.17 billion of TTV, up 22%, revenue of A$204.6 million, up 20%, and EBITDA of A$94.0 million, up 21%, with a 45.9% EBITDA margin. That is not a broken travel retailer. That is a marketplace with operating leverage.

The stock has sold off anyway. The liquid ASX line recently traded around A$2.39, while the OTC WEBJF quote can be stale and illiquid. Using the ASX price and roughly 0.72 AUD/USD, the economic current price is about US$1.72 per WEBJF equivalent share, not the stale US$0.93 print that still appears on some OTC screens.

At A$2.39, with roughly 362 million shares, equity value is about A$865 million. Against FY26 guidance for underlying EBITDA of A$147 million to A$155 million, the stock trades at roughly 9 to 10x forward underlying earnings and about 4x EV/EBITDA after giving credit for net cash and the convertible-note transition. That is cheap for a business growing TTV more than 20%, holding TTV margin at about 6.5%, generating high cash conversion, and guiding FY27 bookings to remain double-digit with stable margins even after the Spanish audit disclosure.

The optical trap is accounting and timing. Statutory NPAT looks messier because of acquired amortization, share-based payments, non-operating marks, standalone post-demerger costs, and convertible-note finance costs. The clean engine is simpler: WebBeds is scaling revenue at a stable take rate, converting that into EBITDA margins in the mid-40s, and investing in contracting, mapping, content, conversion, and AI pricing to push toward the FY30 A$10 billion TTV target.

That FY30 target is the torque. If WebBeds reaches A$10 billion TTV and holds a 6.5% revenue/TTV margin, revenue power is about A$650 million. At a 44% to 47% EBITDA margin before corporate costs, that implies roughly A$285 million to A$305 million WebBeds EBITDA, or perhaps A$255 million to A$275 million group EBITDA after central costs. Today’s enterprise value is only a little over 2x that possible FY30 group EBITDA. The market is pricing the business as if recent growth will fade quickly, not as if WebBeds is compounding share in a global distribution niche.

The bear case is real but visible. A travel downturn, Middle East disruption, competitive discounting by Hotelbeds or Expedia-like supply platforms, or a material Spanish tax liability could compress the multiple. AI is also an investor fear: if large OTAs, agents, or hotel chains use AI to bypass intermediaries, the bedbank layer could lose bargaining power. I think that risk is over-discounted near the current price because WebBeds’ value is not just search. It is contracted supply, rates, availability, credit, localization, API plumbing, reconciliation, and a global buyer network. AI may improve conversion and pricing for WebBeds before it displaces the marketplace.

The strongest near-term catalyst is the FY26 result due 27 May 2026. If management confirms guidance, shows no deterioration in TTV margin, and gives credible FY27 double-digit booking commentary, the stock can re-rate quickly because expectations are now low. Resolution or containment of the Spanish audit would also remove a major overhang. A cleaner post-convertible balance sheet and renewed capital return would help as well.

Bear target: A$1.80, about US$1.30, down 25%. This assumes growth slows, EBITDA falls toward A$130 million, the Spanish tax issue consumes value, and the market keeps WEB at a distressed 4 to 5x EBITDA.

Base target: A$4.30, about US$3.10, up 80%. This assumes FY26 guidance is met, FY27 EBITDA grows into the A$170 million to A$180 million range, and the market pays 7.5 to 8x EBITDA for a clean B2B marketplace with net cash and double-digit booking growth.

Bull target: A$6.20, about US$4.46, up 159%. This assumes WebBeds keeps taking share, FY27/FY28 EBITDA visibility rises above A$200 million, the Spanish issue proves manageable, and investors begin underwriting the FY30 A$10 billion TTV path at a 9 to 10x EBITDA multiple.

At today’s primary-line price of about A$2.39, or US$1.72 equivalent, WEBJF is a misunderstood demerger stock whose business quality improved faster than its label changed. The upside case is not that travel recovers. It already did. The upside case is that a high-margin B2B travel marketplace is being priced like a stale, messy OTA.
